planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; blooms on old wood — do not prune until after bloom or you will remove next year's flower buds.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; oakleaf hydrangea is the premier four-season native flowering shrub for shade, offering showy white cone-shaped flower panicles in early summer that fade gracefully to dusty pink and tan and persist on the plant, then brilliant orange-red-purple fall foliage, and finally striking cinnamon-brown exfoliating bark on thick, architectural stems all winter. It is native to shaded ravines and woodland slopes in the Southeast. The shrub blooms on old wood — do not prune unless absolutely necessary, and only immediately after bloom. Named cultivars like 'Snow Queen' (large upright blooms), 'Pee Wee' (compact), and 'Ruby Slippers' (pink flowers) are widely available and all perform well in zone 8–9 gardens.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; this hydrangea blooms on new wood — cut it back to 12 inches in late winter for the largest, most vigorous flowers.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; smooth hydrangea is the native parent of the enormously popular cultivars 'Annabelle' and 'Incrediball', which have transformed shade gardens with their enormous round white flower heads up to 12 inches across. Unlike oakleaf hydrangea, smooth hydrangea blooms on new wood — cut all stems to 12–18 inches in late winter for the strongest, most floriferous performance. It grows naturally along stream banks, woodland edges, and cliff faces across the eastern US and tolerates more shade and competition than most shrubs. If flower heads become too heavy for stems, cut back less severely the following year. Pollinators of many species use the tiny fertile flowers at the center of the flower heads.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; also sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification. Plants may also be divided in fall.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; fringed bleeding heart is the native eastern species with deeply cut, ferny blue-green foliage and pendant rosy-pink heart-shaped flowers that bloom from late spring through fall — an exceptionally long season for a shade perennial, unlike the common garden bleeding heart (Dicentra spectabilis) which dies back completely after spring bloom. It is native to rocky woodland slopes in the Appalachians from New York to Georgia. The foliage may die back partially in summer heat but rebounds with fall cooling. It spreads slowly by self-seeding to naturalize a shaded bed and is an outstanding companion for hostas, ferns, and native wildflowers. All parts are toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade; slow to establish from division but once settled forms a dense weed-suppressing mat. Purchase only from nursery-propagated sources.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; heartleaf wild ginger is the evergreen southeastern counterpart to the deciduous Canadian wild ginger (Asarum canadense), forming a dense, low mat of glossy, evergreen heart-shaped leaves that is one of the finest shade groundcovers available for southern gardens. The curious brown jug-shaped flowers (earning the folk name 'little brown jugs') bloom at ground level in early spring, hidden beneath the leaves, and are pollinated by small ground-crawling insects. The aromatic rhizomes smell and taste of ginger. It colonizes slowly from rhizomes to fill a shaded bed over several years. Plant under oaks, maples, or at the base of large shrubs in woodland gardens. All parts mildly toxic if consumed in quantity.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; this is a native alternative to the invasive Asian burning bush (E. alatus).

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; eastern wahoo is a native alternative to the invasive Asian burning bush (Euonymus alatus) and performs the same landscape function — stunning fall color — while supporting native wildlife instead of escaping into woodlands. In fall, the foliage turns brilliant scarlet-red while the seed pods split open to reveal dangling bright coral-pink and orange arillate seeds that attract migratory birds including thrushes, bluebirds, and waxwings. The purple-tinged flowers in late spring are modest but charming. The plant tolerates deep shade and is one of few shrubs that thrives under the dense canopy of mature oaks and maples. All parts are toxic to humans and livestock. This is the true 'burning bush' of the native landscape.
